raw_case:
Low-dose fenfluramine as an effective treatment option for ‘atypical’ Dravet syndrome


Highlights

•Dravet syndrome (DS) is diagnosed based on the clinical course and genetic testing.•Low-dose fenfluramine (FFA) is an effective treatment for typical and atypical DS.•FFA caused no serious adverse events in the present case of atypical DS.Dravet syndrome (DS) is characterized by recurrent convulsive seizures, including status epilepticus, and intellectual disability as a comorbidity. Seizures associated with DS are commonly resistant to antiseizure medications. Typical features of DS are recurrent episodes of status epilepticus, the presence of genetic mutations, and no abnormal magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) findings. Here, we report a rare case of DS in a 14-year-old girl who was negative for genetic mutations, had experienced status epilepticus only once, and had abnormal findings on brain MRI. Although our patient’s case features are atypical of DS, they do not contradict the diagnostic criteria. Despite the difficulty in diagnosing DS because of the negative genetic testing results, we started our patient on fenfluramine (FFA). Long-term treatment with low-dose FFA effectively controlled our patient’s seizures and resulted in cognitive and functional improvements.1

Introduction

Dravet syndrome (DS) is a rare form of epilepsy characterized by recurrent convulsive seizures, including status epilepticus, and mild to moderate intellectual disability as a comorbidity [1]. Individuals with DS often have seizures that are resistant to anticonvulsant medications [2]. Tonic-clonic or clonic febrile seizures generally begin in infancy, although infants with DS usually exhibit typical development until around 1 year of age [3]. Status epilepticus is more frequent in children younger than school age [3]. Beyond the age of 5 years, the frequency of seizures starts to decrease, but status epilepticus and unexpected death from DS-related epilepsy result in a higher mortality rate than among individuals with other types of epilepsy [4]. Further, the frequency of seizures results in a significant burden for those with DS and drives cognitive dysfunction [5]. As such, controlling seizures in those with DS is imperative.Since 2020, increased attention has focused on the use of fenfluramine (FFA), which was once marketed as an appetite suppressant [6], for the treatment of seizures in DS. Here, we present an atypical case of DS in a 14-year-old girl in whom FFA was remarkably effective.2

Case presentation

In this case, the patient’s epilepsy started with an afebrile convulsion at 3 months of age. Thereafter, convulsive seizures were easily provoked by high fever and were resistant to antiseizure medications. The patient experienced convulsive status epilepticus (lasting ∼ 30 min) only once at 11 months of age. The patient did not exhibit any significant developmental delays in infancy, with delays only recognized after 1 year of age. Specifically, the patient took her first steps at the age of 2 years and 3 months and started to clearly use single words at the age of 3 years and 11 months. The patient experienced only febrile or afebrile generalized tonic-clonic seizures (GTCS) and did not develop any other seizure types.Brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) at 1 year of age revealed bilateral multiple periventricular nodular heterotopia (PVNH) without cortical heterotopia (Fig. 1). The patient experienced an episode of acute encephalopathy at 5 years of age, which left her with cognitive and motor regression. The patient lost the ability to communicate and required support to maintain a standing position. No mutations were detected in the sodium voltage-gated channel alpha subunit 1 (SCN1A), protocadherin 19 (PCDH19), and filamin genes at 5 years of age. Different medications were tried to control the patient’s seizures, including valproic acid (VPA), carbamazepine, phenobarbital, zonisamide, levetiracetam, perampanel, clobazam (CLB), clonazepam, nitrazepam, gabapentin, topiramate (TPM), stiripentol, and potassium bromide (KBr), but all were ineffective.At her first visit to the National Epilepsy Center at 11 years of age, the patient was having 20 seizures per week and was being treated with KBr 19 mg/kg/day, VPA 19 mg/kg/day, CLB 0.2 mg/kg/day, lamotrigine (LTG) 1.5 mg/kg/day, and TPM 8.6 mg/kg/day. An electroencephalography (EEG) sleep record prior to the initiation of FFA revealed a burst of bilateral frontal dominant sharp waves (Fig. 2A). We considered a diagnosis of DS due to the presence of intractable convulsive seizures that were easily provoked by high fever. Because discontinuation of LTG did not improve the seizures, we started treatment with FFA 0.2 mg/kg/day. The patient was seizure free for 6 weeks, but experienced somnolence. Decreasing the dose of FFA to 0.12 mg/kg/day improved her somnolence, and the patient remained seizure free for 3 months. Treatment with low-dose FFA (0.12 mg/kg/day) for 7 months markedly improved the EEG sleep record (Fig. 2B). Subsequently, KBr was gradually discontinued, after which the patient regained the ability to communicate verbally and was able to walk alone.After a 1-year seizure-free period, the patient experienced a relapse of convulsive seizures. The dose of FFA was increased to 0.15 mg/kg/day, and although the seizures occurred monthly, they were much weaker than prior to the introduction of FFA. After starting on FFA, the patient’s GTCS lasting 1 min were superseded by a tonic seizure lasting 10 s, followed by several clonic jerks. The patient experienced no adverse events on FFA except for transient diarrhea and somnolence. A cardiac ultrasound examination 6 months after initiating FFA was normal, and low-dose (0.12 mg/kg/day) FFA was not associated with any adverse events.3

Discussion

In our patient, MRI revealed PVNH. In DS, neuroradiological studies usually do not show specific abnormal findings [7], and PVNH is rarely reported [8]. Furthermore, there is usually no correlation between the presence of MRI abnormalities and either the age at seizure onset or seizure duration [9]. In our patient, PVNH was not considered to be the cause of epilepsy given the lack of clinico-electrical correlation. However, Guerrini et al [9] previously reported abnormal findings related to DS usually only observed in patients without SCN1A mutations. Although it is difficult to completely rule out PVNH as the cause of epilepsy in our patient, her seizures were easily provoked by a high fever, which is a characteristic feature of DS. The clinical features of our patient were consistent with those of DS, except for the presence of PVNH and the absence of an SCN1A mutation, and so we consider this to be a case of atypical DS.Based on the 2022 International League Against Epilepsy (ILAE) classification and definition of epilepsy syndromes [10], episodes of statues epilepticus are common in DS before 5 years of age. Although recurrent episodes of status epilepticus are common in DS [3], our patient experienced only one episode of status epilepticus (at 11 months of age), which is atypical for DS. However, recurrent episodes of status epilepticus are not mandatory for a diagnosis of DS [10]; rather, recurrent intractable convulsive seizures that are easily provoked by fever are more important for the diagnosis of DS [11], [12], and our patient met this criterion.No gene mutations were detected in our patient. It has been reported that only 20 % of patients with DS are negative for gene mutations, and that 80–85 % of patients with DS have a pathogenic variant in the SCN1A gene [13]. However, a diagnosis of DS should not be made on the basis of positive gene mutation test results, and, conversely, a diagnosis of DS should not be ruled out in the absence of gene mutations [14]. Relying on the presence of gene mutations to diagnose DS could lead to delays in precise treatment and a poor clinical course for patients with DS.Overall, the diagnosis of DS in our patient was challenging due to the presence of abnormal findings on brain MRI, negative genetic testing results, and the lack of multiple episodes of status epilepticus, all of which could be considered ‘atypical’ features of DS. However, these features do not contradict the 2022 ILAE diagnostic criteria for DS, in which a positive gene mutation result is classified as ‘optional’ for the diagnosis of DS [10].We diagnosed our patient as having DS and decided to start her on FFA, which resulted in good clinical outcomes with a long seizure-free period, as well as improvements in cognitive and motor function associated with the discontinuation of ineffective medications, such as LTG, KBr, and TPM. DS can be classified as a developmental and epileptic encephalopathy [3]. Because seizure frequency decreased and there were marked improvements in EEG findings after initiating FFA, we consider that our patient also experienced improvements in cognitive and functional aspects after treatment for epileptic encephalopathy.4

Conclusion

We propose that FFA is an effective treatment for DS with atypical features in patients experiencing recurrent seizures that are easily provoked by high fever from infancy, together with intellectual disabilities, regardless of the presence of genetic mutations or multiple episodes of status epilepticus. Furthermore, this case demonstrates that a differential diagnosis of DS should be considered in patients with both intractable recurrent convulsive seizures that are easily provoked by fever and intellectual disabilities, even if other clinical features are atypical of DS, and treatment with FFA initiated. A comprehensive diagnosis is important in patients with epilepsy syndromes.

Ethical statement

Ethical review was waived for this study due to its case report nature, and informed consent was obtained from the parents.

Funding sources

This work was supported by the Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare Research program on rare and intractable diseases (Grant no. JPMH23FC0201).

CRediT authorship contribution statement

Akihiro Iguchi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation. Tokito Yamaguchi: Validation. Tomona Yabe: Validation. Mitsuhiro Miyashita: Validation. Satoshi Mizutani: Validation. Hideyuki Otani: Validation. Rie Miyata: Validation. Katsumi Imai: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

Declaration of competing interest

The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information**: 14-year-old girl.
- **Chief Complaint**: Recurrent seizures.
- **History of Present Illness**: 
  - Initial presentation with afebrile convulsion at 3 months of age.
  - Subsequent convulsive seizures triggered by high fever, resistant to antiseizure medications.
  - One episode of status epilepticus lasting ~30 minutes at 11 months of age.
  - Developmental delays noted after 1 year of age: first steps at 2 years 3 months, first single words at 3 years 11 months.
  - Cognitive and motor regression following an acute encephalopathy episode at 5 years of age.
- **Past Medical History**: Trial of multiple antiseizure medications (valproic acid, carbamazepine, phenobarbital, zonisamide, levetiracetam, perampanel, clobazam, clonazepam, nitrazepam, gabapentin, topiramate, stiripentol, potassium bromide) was ineffective.
- **Personal History**: None documented.
- **Family History**: None documented.
- **Physical Examination**: Not specified.
- **Ancillary Tests**: 
  - Brain MRI (1 year of age): bilateral multiple periventricular nodular heterotopia (PVNH) without cortical heterotopia.
  - Genetic testing (5 years of age): negative for SCN1A, PCDH19, and filamin mutations.
  - EEG (prior to FFA): bilateral frontal dominant sharp wave bursts; improved significantly after FFA treatment.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Initial Considerations**:
  - Dravet Syndrome (DS) due to intractable seizures provoked by fever and intellectual disability.
  - Epilepsy secondary to MRI-detected PVNH, though no clinico-electrical correlation was identified.
  - Atypical DS considered due to negative genetic testing and lack of recurrent status epilepticus episodes.

- **Inclusion and Exclusion of Diagnoses**:
  - **Dravet Syndrome**: Despite atypical features (negative genetic testing, one episode of status epilepticus, and MRI abnormalities), recurrent febrile-induced convulsive seizures and intellectual disability fulfill diagnostic criteria per 2022 ILAE guidelines.
  - **Epilepsy due to MRI abnormalities (PVNH)**: Excluded as MRI findings were not demonstrably linked to seizure activity nor the clinical presentation; PVNH is also rarely reported in DS.
  - **Other Epilepsy Syndromes**: Considered but less likely, given the unique combination of symptoms and non-responsiveness to a broad range of antiseizure medications.
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
Atypical Dravet Syndrome with bilateral periventricular nodular heterotopia on MRI, negative SCN1A mutation, and a single episode of status epilepticus. Diagnosis supported by clinical history of febrile-provoked convulsive seizures, intellectual disability, and seizure resistance to multiple medications. 

Treatment with low-dose fenfluramine (0.12–0.15 mg/kg/day) substantially reduced seizure frequency, improved cognitive and motor function, and was well tolerated.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's try to work through this case step by step. The patient is a 14-year-old girl with a history of recurrent seizures starting from infancy. Let me start by listing out the key points from the case summary.

First, her initial seizure was an afebrile convulsion at 3 months old. That's pretty early. Then she had more seizures triggered by high fever, and they were resistant to multiple antiseizure meds. She had status epilepticus at 11 months, which lasted about 30 minutes. Then developmental delays became noticeable after age 1—like walking late and talking late. Then at age 5, after an acute encephalopathy episode, she had cognitive and motor regression. Her brain MRI at 1 year showed bilateral periventricular nodular heterotopia (PVNH) without cortical involvement. Genetic tests came back negative for SCN1A, PCDH19, and filamin mutations. EEG showed frontal sharp waves that improved with FFA treatment. She's tried a ton of meds without success.

So, starting with the seizures. Early onset, afebrile initially, then febrile triggers. But they're drug-resistant. The developmental delays and regression point to a progressive neurological disorder. The PVNH on MRI is a key finding. Periventricular nodular heterotopia is a neuronal migration disorder. Usually, FLNA mutations are a common cause of X-linked PVNH, but her test was negative. Other genes associated with PVNH include ARFGEF2 and others. But since FLNA was negative, maybe it's another genetic cause.

The genetic tests she had were for SCN1A, which is linked to Dravet syndrome, and PCDH19, which causes epilepsy with intellectual disability, especially in females. Those came back negative. So maybe not those. But Dravet typically starts with febrile seizures, and her initial seizure was afebrile. Hmm. But Dravet can also have later developmental issues. But the MRI showing PVNH isn't typical for Dravet. So maybe another syndrome.

The fact that her EEG improved with FFA (maybe fenfluramine?) which is used in Dravet and some other epilepsies. But again, genetic testing for SCN1A was negative. There's also the possibility of a different genetic mutation that wasn't tested here. For example, some other ion channel gene or a gene related to synaptic function.

The PVNH without cortical heterotopia. So the heterotopia is periventricular. FLNA mutations are a big cause, but since that's negative, maybe autosomal recessive forms. For example, mutations in ARFGEF2 can cause PVNH with microcephaly and other issues. But the case doesn't mention microcephaly. Alternatively, there are other genes like NEDD4L or others.

The developmental regression after acute encephalopathy at age 5. That makes me think of a metabolic disorder or a mitochondrial disorder. Some inborn errors of metabolism can present with seizures and developmental regression, especially after an illness. But the presence of PVNH might point more towards a malformation rather than a metabolic issue. However, some metabolic disorders can also have structural brain abnormalities.

Another angle: drug-resistant epilepsy with PVNH. There's a condition called Ehlers-Danlos syndrome periventricular heterotopia, but that's linked to FLNA. Since FLNA is negative, maybe another gene. Also, CDKL5 or ARX mutations can cause early seizures and developmental issues, but CDKL5 is more associated with Rett-like features. ARX mutations are linked to X-linked lissencephaly with ambiguous genitalia, but that might not fit here.

Wait, the EEG showed frontal dominant sharp waves. Frontal lobe epilepsy? PVNH can be associated with focal seizures. The improvement with FFA might indicate serotonergic mechanisms, as fenfluramine affects serotonin release.

Another thought: the combination of PVNH and epilepsy might suggest a tubulinopathy or a tubulin-related gene mutation. Tubulin genes are involved in neuronal migration. But tubulinopathies often present with a range of cortical malformations, like lissencephaly or polymicrogyria, which aren't mentioned here.

Alternatively, the patient might have a mutation in the gene NPRL3, which is part of the GATOR1 complex. Mutations here can cause focal epilepsy with PVNH. GATOR1-related epilepsy is often drug-resistant and can present with heterotopia. That's a possibility, especially if previous genetic tests didn't cover those genes.

Another condition to consider is PCDH19-related epilepsy. Even though the test was negative, maybe there's a mosaic mutation or another variant not detected. But the negative result makes this less likely.

Wait, the MRI at 1 year showed PVNH without cortical heterotopia. FLNA-related PVNH is typically X-linked dominant, and females can have variable presentations. But if the genetic test was negative, maybe it's a different gene. There's also the possibility of a somatic mutation, but that's less common.

Putting this together: Drug-resistant epilepsy starting in infancy, PVNH, developmental delays and regression, negative for common genetic causes. Maybe a rare genetic epilepsy syndrome associated with PVNH. Given the improvement with FFA, which is used in Dravet and Lennox-Gastaut, but Dravet is SCN1A, which was negative. Maybe a different ion channelopathy.

Alternatively, the PVNH could be a clue. Since FLNA is ruled out, perhaps mutations in other genes like ARFGEF2, which is autosomal recessive. But ARFGEF2-related PVNH is associated with microcephaly and severe epilepsy. If she doesn't have microcephaly, maybe not. Or maybe another gene.

Another possibility is a mutation in the gene EZH2, which is linked to Weaver syndrome and sometimes PVNH. But that's more associated with overgrowth.

Wait, the patient's developmental regression after acute encephalopathy at 5 years. Could that be due to a mitochondrial disorder? Mitochondrial encephalopathies can present with seizures, developmental regression, and normal MRI initially, but PVNH isn't a typical finding. However, some mitochondrial disorders can have structural abnormalities. But the MRI here shows PVNH, which is a migration defect, not typically mitochondrial.

Alternatively, a leukodystrophy? But the MRI findings don't mention white matter changes.

Another angle: the combination of PVNH and epilepsy. A recent study mentioned that mutations in the gene SLC35A2 can cause PVNH with epilepsy. SLC35A2 is involved in glycosylation and is part of congenital disorders of glycosylation. These can present with seizures, developmental delay, and heterotopia. CDG type II, perhaps. But would the genetic tests have checked this? The tests mentioned were for SCN1A, PCDH19, and filamin. SLC35A2 is an X-linked gene. If she has a mutation here, that could explain the PVNH and epilepsy. Also, some CDG types can present with drug-resistant seizures and developmental regression.

Alternatively, mutations in the gene NEDD4L have been associated with periventricular heterotopia and epilepsy. NEDD4L is involved in neuronal development. However, this is a newer association and might not be included in standard genetic panels.

Given the extensive list of tried medications without success, including stiripentol and bromide, which are used in Dravet and other refractory epilepsies, but no response, perhaps the underlying etiology is a rare genetic disorder not covered by the initial genetic tests.

Putting it all together, the most likely diagnosis is a genetic epilepsy syndrome associated with periventricular nodular heterotopia due to a mutation in a gene not previously tested, such as those in the GATOR1 complex (DEPDC5, NPRL2, NPRL3) or SLC35A2. Given the presence of PVNH and drug-resistant epilepsy, and the negative FLNA, SCN1A, and PCDH19 tests, the leading diagnosis might be DEPDC5-related epilepsy. DEPDC5 mutations are associated with focal epilepsy and brain malformations like heterotopia. However, another possibility is a mutation in the gene SLC35A2 causing a congenital disorder of glycosylation with PVNH and epilepsy.

But without more specific test results, based on the available information, the most fitting diagnosis would be a genetic epilepsy related to periventricular heterotopia, likely due to a mutation in a gene associated with the mTOR pathway, such as DEPDC5, NPRL3, or related genes. These mutations lead to mTOR pathway dysregulation, which can cause cortical malformations and drug-resistant epilepsy. The mTOR pathway is involved in cell growth and migration, so mutations here can lead to heterotopia and seizures. This fits with the PVNH and refractory epilepsy. Therefore, the final diagnosis would be related to a mutation in the GATOR1 complex genes causing mTORopathy.
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient’s early-onset afebrile and febrile seizures, drug-resistant epilepsy, and developmental delays suggest a genetic epilepsy syndrome. The MRI finding of bilateral periventricular nodular heterotopia (PVNH) points to a neuronal migration disorder. FLNA mutations, a common cause of X-linked PVNH, were ruled out.  
<step 2> Negative results for SCN1A (Dravet syndrome) and PCDH19 exclude these etiologies. The lack of cortical heterotopia narrows the focus to isolated PVNH-associated genetic causes, such as mTOR pathway dysregulation (e.g., GATOR1 complex genes: DEPDC5, NPRL2/3) or glycosylation disorders (e.g., SLC35A2).  
<step 3> Developmental regression post-encephalopathy at age 5 and EEG improvement with fenfluramine (FFA) hint at serotonergic or mTOR pathway involvement. FFA’s efficacy in mTOR-related epilepsy and drug resistance aligns with GATOR1 mutations, which disrupt mTOR signaling, causing cortical malformations and intractable seizures.  
<step 4> While metabolic or mitochondrial disorders could explain regression, the structural PVNH favors a malformative/mTORopathy etiology. Genetic testing gaps (e.g., DEPDC5, NPRL3, SLC35A2) likely explain prior negative results.  

### Answer:
GATOR1-complex related epilepsy (e.g., DEPDC5, NPRL2/3 mutation) with periventricular nodular heterotopia.
